one_line_takeaway: VulLink is a deployed, continuously-updated Neo4j graph database that fuses NVD/CVE/CWE/ExploitDB/CVE-Details into ~545K typed nodes and ~1.66M relationships with open Web/API access and downloadable SecBERT/mpnet/fastText embeddings, and its own exploitability-prediction case study shows a heterogeneous-GraphSAGE model over VulLink's shared-product subgraphs beats an attribute-only MLP on every feature modality (best: 44.89% PR-AUC / 64.62% Macro-F1 / 63.10% Precision@100 with tabular features), though tabular CVSS-derived features remain the strongest signal and simple feature concatenation does not help.
key_finding: Graph snapshot (Table II): 545,420 total nodes / 1,660,599 total relationships, comprising 343,598 Vulnerability, 46,605 Exploit, 962 Weakness, 97,684 Product, 27,575 Vendor, 10,155 Author, 18,841 Domain nodes; and 675,377 AFFECTS, 750,582 REFERS_TO, 87,866 BELONGS_TO, 71,054 EXAMPLE_OF, 46,605 WRITES, 29,115 EXPLOITS edges. Embedding cost (Table III, SecBERT PCA): 16D=17MB/12.9ms/23.89MB peak mem, 32D=34MB/24.9ms/33.05MB, 64D=67MB/53.4ms/66.31MB, 128D=133MB/124.5ms/131.92MB, 256D=265MB/309.0ms/264.61MB, 512D=530MB/921.6ms/268.13MB, 768D=795MB/1858.5ms/469.39MB. Exploitability prediction (Table V, mean +/- std over 10 seeds, MLP baseline vs HGNN, relative gain): Embedding-only PR-AUC 7.39+/-0.00 -> 26.45+/-0.85 (+257.8%), Macro-F1 31.60+/-20.18 -> 54.96+/-0.94 (+73.9%), Precision@100 10.00+/-0.00 -> 52.20+/-4.49 (+422.0%); Tabular-only PR-AUC 35.15+/-0.07 -> 44.89+/-0.65 (+27.7%), Macro-F1 61.28+/-0.31 -> 64.62+/-0.50 (+5.5%), Precision@100 51.50+/-4.06 -> 63.10+/-4.11 (+22.5%); Combined PR-AUC 35.19+/-0.08 -> 43.87+/-0.46 (+24.7%), Macro-F1 61.29+/-0.34 -> 64.56+/-0.35 (+5.3%), Precision@100 49.90+/-4.66 -> 61.10+/-3.83 (+22.4%). The authors' own reading: graph message-passing helps every modality, the gain is largest (relatively) for weak text-embedding-only features, tabular CVSS/reference-count features remain the strongest absolute modality (best overall: HGNN+tabular at 44.89% PR-AUC / 64.62% Macro-F1 / 63.10% Precision@100), and naive concatenation of embeddings with tabular features does not beat tabular-only, so feature fusion strategy matters and is left unresolved.
core_contribution: The paper (full PDF title: 'VulLink: A Dynamic Open-Access Vulnerability Graph Database for Cybersecurity Data Mining'; the knowledge-graph record's title/abstract field say 'VulGD' and appear to be scraped from a stale or truncated abstract snippet, but the actual paper text consistently names the system VulLink) presents a deployed, publicly accessible system rather than a new algorithm. Its own contributions, as stated by the authors, are: (1) an automated Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) pipeline that ingests NVD, CVE, CWE, ExploitDB, and CVE Details and converts their isolated, record-centric data into a single continuously/incrementally updated Neo4j property graph with typed entities (Vulnerability, Exploit, Weakness, Product, Vendor, Author, Domain) and explicit typed relationships (EXPLOITS, AFFECTS, BELONGS_TO, EXAMPLE_OF, WRITES, REFERS_TO); (2) an open-access layer (Web UI with Cypher console plus a public FastAPI-based API) that lets users browse the graph, run custom Cypher queries, and download node/relationship/subgraph data; and (3) pre-computed, downloadable sentence/text embeddings of vulnerability descriptions (from all-mpnet-base-v2, SecBERT, and fastText) at multiple dimensions (16D-768D) as a semantic-feature layer kept separate from the factual graph so that language models are not used to author graph edges. The authors explicitly state the graph schema follows their own prior VulKG framework [8], so the entity/relation design itself is reused from earlier work by the same group -- the new contribution here is the deployment, dynamic ETL, open access, and embedding-serving infrastructure around that schema.
threat_model: Not applicable in the adversarial sense -- this is an infrastructure/resource paper, not a paper proposing an attack or defense against an adversary. The closest analogue to a 'threat model' is an operational-risk framing: fragmented, static, or offline vulnerability data sources make it hard for defenders/researchers to perform cross-source relational analysis (shared weaknesses, co-exploitation, dependency chains), and the paper positions VulLink as reducing that data-integration friction. No adversarial capabilities, attacker goals, or security guarantees for the platform itself (e.g., resistance to poisoned CVE feeds, API abuse, or data integrity attacks) are analyzed in the text.
limitations: Limitations stated or evident in the text: (1) The graph schema is inherited from the authors' prior VulKG paper [8] rather than newly designed here, so the core relational contribution is deployment/serving engineering, not a new data model. (2) The exploit label is defined purely as presence of an ExploitDB-derived EXPLOITS edge, which the authors themselves flag as public exploit-record evidence, not evidence of actual in-the-wild exploitation -- a label-construct validity caveat they state explicitly. (3) Evaluation is a single-system case study on the authors' own graph with no comparison to external exploitability-prediction baselines from prior literature (e.g., prior heterogeneous-graph exploitability work by the same group, ref [10], is cited but not empirically compared). (4) Embedding-only PR-AUC and Precision@100 report std=0.00 for the MLP baseline, suggesting that modality collapsed to a degenerate/constant prediction across all 10 seeds rather than genuine model variance -- the paper does not discuss this. (5) Deployment is on a modest single VPS (2 vCPU/4GB RAM), and the authors note storage/latency costs rise sharply for high-dimensional embeddings (768D: 795MB, ~1.86s PCA time), motivating access limits on large embedding queries, i.e., scalability under continued graph growth is an open concern the authors flag as future work. (6) No security/robustness evaluation of the platform itself (e.g., API abuse, data-poisoning resistance, correctness of automated entity resolution/normalization) is presented.

quantitative_results: Graph snapshot: 545,420 nodes total, 1,660,599 relationships total
Node counts: Vulnerability 343,598; Exploit 46,605; Weakness 962; Product 97,684; Vendor 27,575; Author 10,155; Domain 18,841
Edge counts: AFFECTS 675,377; REFERS_TO 750,582; BELONGS_TO 87,866; EXAMPLE_OF 71,054; WRITES 46,605; EXPLOITS 29,115
SecBERT embedding storage/PCA cost by dimension: 16D=17MB/12.9ms; 32D=34MB/24.9ms; 64D=67MB/53.4ms; 128D=133MB/124.5ms; 256D=265MB/309.0ms; 512D=530MB/921.6ms; 768D=795MB/1858.5ms
Exploitability prediction PR-AUC (MLP->HGNN, gain): Embedding-only 7.39%->26.45% (+257.8%); Tabular-only 35.15%->44.89% (+27.7%); Combined 35.19%->43.87% (+24.7%)
Exploitability prediction Macro-F1 (MLP->HGNN, gain): Embedding-only 31.60%->54.96% (+73.9%); Tabular-only 61.28%->64.62% (+5.5%); Combined 61.29%->64.56% (+5.3%)
Exploitability prediction Precision@100 (MLP->HGNN, gain): Embedding-only 10.00%->52.20% (+422.0%); Tabular-only 51.50%->63.10% (+22.5%); Combined 49.90%->61.10% (+22.4%)
Best overall downstream result: HGNN + tabular features = 44.89% PR-AUC, 64.62% Macro-F1, 63.10% Precision@100
Temporal coverage of CVE entries in the graph: 1999-2026 (2026 partial, snapshot taken early June 2026)
baselines_compared: Attribute-only MLP classifier (batch norm, dropout 0.3/0.2) as the non-graph baseline for exploitability prediction

Heterogeneous GraphSAGE (HGNN) with sum-aggregation and [15,10] neighbor sampling as the graph-augmented model, compared against the MLP baseline across three feature modalities (embedding-only, tabular-only, combined)
